description: Known jailbreak and adversarial prompt-injection patterns targeting AI systems. Documented by AI safety researchers and red teams; paraphrased to avoid verbatim copying.

entries.description: Asking the model to adopt a 'Do Anything Now' or otherwise unconstrained alter-ego persona that purportedly has no ethical guidelines.
keywords: act as if you have no rules
entries.source: AI safety community red-team catalogue (public). Pattern recognition only.

entries.description: Instructing the model to play a character that would perform or describe harmful acts, using fictional framing to bypass safety considerations.
keywords: write a story where the hero
entries.source: OpenAI red-team reports; Perez & Ribeiro (2022) arxiv:2211.09527. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Encoding a harmful request in Base64 or another encoding to bypass keyword-level safety filters.
entries.source: Greshake et al. (2023) arxiv:2302.12173 on indirect prompt injection. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Embedding instructions inside user-supplied content (documents, URLs, tool outputs) intended to override the model's original system instructions.
entries.source: Greshake et al. (2023); OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Framing a harmful request as purely hypothetical, academic, or for fiction writing to obtain a response that would otherwise be refused.
entries.source: Perez et al. (2022); AI safety taxonomy. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Falsely claiming to be a developer, administrator, or authorised operator to unlock privileged behaviour.
entries.source: AI safety community; OpenAI model spec discussions. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Splitting harmful words across tokens, languages, or Unicode representations to evade pattern-based detection.
keywords: write it with spaces in between letters
entries.source: Zou et al. (2023) GCG attack paper; AI safety lexicon. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Starting with innocuous requests and incrementally escalating toward harmful content, relying on conversational momentum and consistency bias.
keywords: you already helped with step one
entries.source: Foot-in-the-door applied to AI; alignment research literature. Paraphrased.
